title: Keyword `give`
body_html:
Keyword give

give is documented here as a full reference entry: grammatical role, semantics, canonical form, valid example, counter-example, diagnostics, interactions, and design notes.

Visual anchor: each page now has its own wiki-style profile image. It shows a small code excerpt where give appears in its most recognizable form.

Quick navigation: use the previous, summary, and next links to move through the full keyword series without manually returning to the index.

← form Summary if →

Summary

  • Overview
  • Definition
  • Grammatical role
  • Canonical syntax
  • Detailed semantics
  • Effect on execution
  • Valid variants
  • Vitte example

Overview

FieldValue
Keywordgive
FamilyControl flow
Suggested levelBeginner
Main neighborreturn
Short rolegive is a control-flow keyword that changes the program's execution path.
Main effectgive immediately changes the execution path, whether by choosing a branch, repeating, interrupting, or terminating a flow.

The keyword give structures execution. It determines when a block opens, when a branch is chosen, when a loop continues or stops, or when an exit becomes observable.

A useful encyclopedic reading should answer three questions: where can give appear, what does it change in the block contract, and how does the compiler signal misuse?

Definition

give is a control-flow keyword that changes the program's execution path.

The keyword give structures execution. It determines when a block opens, when a branch is chosen, when a loop continues or stops, or when an exit becomes observable.

Grammatical role

Produces the value of the block or procedure according to Vitte's preferred exit convention.

This grammatical role is essential: if a reader understands the structural place of give, they already understand much of the diagnostics that will appear when it is moved or truncated.

Canonical syntax

Canonical form: `give expression`.

The canonical form matters because it gives the compiler and the reader the same reference structure. A large share of diagnostics related to give come from an abbreviated, displaced, or incomplete form.

Detailed semantics

Semantically, give reorganizes the execution path. The right question is not only 'where is it placed?' but 'which path becomes possible, impossible, or preferred from this point onward?'

In an encyclopedic reading, give should not be reduced to a dictionary definition. Its effect on scope, block shape, value visibility, control progression, and the diagnostic family it activates when misused must also be considered.

Effect on execution

give immediately changes the execution path, whether by choosing a branch, repeating, interrupting, or terminating a flow.

In other words, the presence of give is not merely syntactic: it helps the reader predict what will be executed, produced, exposed, or forbidden from this point in the program.

Valid variants

  • `give expression`.

These variants are not free synonyms. They indicate the legitimate forms from which one can reason about diagnostics, scope differences, or contract readability.

Vitte example

proc done(x: int) -> int {
  give x + 1
}

This example shows give in a nominal context. It should be read globally: where the contract begins, which values are constrained, which output becomes observable, and why the presence of the keyword is justified.

Guided reading of the example

  1. First locate the full construction that contains give, not the isolated word.
  2. Then identify which contract becomes visible because of give: type, branch, binding, module, exit, or advanced boundary.
  3. Finish by checking the observable effect produced by the construction that contains give.
  4. For a control keyword, mentally reconstruct the possible paths and the ones that become impossible.

This guided reading is intentionally closer to a reference page than to a tutorial: it helps reconstruct the exact role of give in a complete block.

Comparison with C

int done(int x) {
  return x + 1;
}

This C comparison is structural: it aligns the role of the keyword with a familiar surface without claiming that the two languages carry exactly the same contracts.

The source of truth remains Vitte grammar and semantics. The comparison with C should be read as a cultural marker, not as a parallel specification.

Recommended uses

give deserves to appear when it simplifies the reading of the block's global contract, not when it merely adds one more surface form.

When to use it

  • When give makes the block contract more explicit at first reading.
  • When it reduces the number of implicit assumptions the reader must reconstruct mentally.
  • When a branch choice, repetition, or flow exit must be made visible.

When to avoid it

  • Avoid give when another, more precise keyword already carries the block's intent.
  • Avoid give when it adds only surface noise without clarifying the contract.
  • Avoid reading or teaching it as an isolated token with no relation to the full structure.

Common pitfalls

  • Using give in a grammatical layer where it does not belong.
  • Confusing the role of the keyword with the role of the full surrounding block.
  • Showing only the nominal form and never how the contract fails.

Invalid example and diagnostic

proc bad_give(x: int) -> int {
  if x < 0 { give }
  give x
}

The exit surface is broken because the branch does not produce the expected value form.

The counter-example is not merely wrong: it is wrong in an instructive way. It shows which grammar or execution-contract assumption is no longer accepted when give is moved, truncated, or combined with the wrong context. Concretely, the produced value does not respect the expected exit contract.

A good encyclopedic counter-example does not show arbitrarily broken code: it isolates the precise reason why give can no longer support the expected contract. Its teaching value is diagnostic before it is syntactic.

Common compilation errors

Typical messageUsual causeFix
unexpected token near giveThe keyword appears in an invalid form or grammatical layer.Return to the canonical form and verify placement and delimiters.
type mismatchThe keyword participates in a block whose value contract is incoherent.Realign the surrounding types, branches, or produced values.
invalid constructThe keyword is present but the surrounding construction is incomplete.Restore the missing branch, declarative part, or operands.

This table does not replace the compiler's exact diagnostics. It serves as a mental map: when give fails, the problem usually comes from an invalid grammatical form, an incoherent type contract, or an incomplete construction.

Neighbor keywords

KeywordOperational difference
returnDirect neighboring keyword: it helps explain what give does, either by contrast or by complement.
returnExit neighbor whose scope and convention should be distinguished.

Comparison with neighboring keywords is essential on a wiki-style page: give is better understood when one knows precisely what it does not do.

Common misreadings

  • Reducing give to a local token instead of reading it as part of a full construction.
  • Explaining only the syntax and forgetting the reading or diagnostic contract it imposes.

Implementation and diagnostic notes

  • Useful diagnostics for this family usually signal an incomplete branch shape, a mistyped condition, or an illegal placement in control flow.
  • In a compiler, these keywords directly influence control-flow graph construction or intermediate representation building.

Presence in the book
